sentences.text: The equipment, and I see that you-- you did a little bit better.
You did it in less than an hour.
So we're going to have a little bit more time to express ourselves and to tell you certain things, which are-- oh, they're very painful, but you have to bring it out.
You know?
It's-- you know, in Hebrew, they have a saying.
[SPEAKING HEBREW] That means that time is very short, and the workload is just huge.
I was born in a small city in Lithuania.
The northern part of-- northern part of Lithuania by the name of Rakiszki.
My date of birth was 2/27/1924.
At that time, my family name was Resnikowitz.
I had two sisters.
One, the older one, which was four years younger than me, Yonina.
And my-- the second one, which was eight years older-- younger than me, and her name was Miriam.
My father's name was Haym, and my mother's name was Lisa.
My mother was born in this town, which was actually a county town, Rakiszki.
My father was born in a different part of Lithuania in a small town by the name of [NON-ENGLISH], which was close to the capital, Kaunas, Kovno.
I had my grandfather that was living at that time.
He was around 60 or 62.
My grandmother died a few years before that.
My parents and my grandfather, they were observant, yes.
I started going in the public school.
And before starting public school, I went to cheder, local in Rakiszki.
Then I went to a [NON-ENGLISH] school, which was a Hebrew school.
And then I went for a short time to [NON-ENGLISH] public school.
And ultimately, when I was nine, I have been sent to live together with my grandparents on my father's side to the capital of Kovno.
And there, I was-- I started in the Hebrew gymnasium, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at the age of nine-- between nine and 10.
My father was a governmental employee.
He-- he was actually by being-- have-- having-- he fought for the independence of Lithuania, and he was given a governmental job, which was a insurance inspector.
And besides that, we also had a factory manufacturing roof paper.
Yeah.
My street address in Rakiszki was [NON-ENGLISH]-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no, [NON-ENGLISH].
That means the [NON-ENGLISH], the street number eight.
As a boy, I belonged to the Boy Scouts.
I belonged to the Hashomer Hatzair.
And ultimately, I belonged to the Betar.
Life was very nice.
As you know, the city, it was not-- it was a small city.
And most of the inhabitants were Jewish.
The Lithuanians, the non-Jews, have lived in the surroundings in the small villages surrounding.
But in Rakiszki, there were, like, 60% or 65% of Jews who lived and who have conducted the business.
And as a young boy, when I grew up, we had a very nice social life.
We had a Jewish bank.

We had different Jewish organizations, organizations who were social organizations, taking care of the less fortunate of those people who were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not too wealthy, and those people who were sick.
We had also a hospital in our city.
And it was a very social and a very nice life.
Especially the Jewish families had a bind between themselves.
We used to visit.
We used to have conferences, lectures.
And in a certain way, life was very desirable.
We had about 3,000-- between 3,000 and 3,500 Jews altogether.
I would say we'd have maybe about [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 150 families.
Yes.
We had very light anti-Semitism.
You could feel that in a certain way.
But it was actually-- Lithuania now was a new country who got their independence in the early '20s.
And Jews have participated very strongly in the liberation and independence of Lithuania.
So there were a certain bind between the Lithuanians and the Jews.
But always there was little strike of anti-Semitism.
Especially when we used to go to Jewish school, we had groups of non-Jewish Lithuanians who used to sometimes call us by name of [NON-ENGLISH], which means the Jew, and so far and so on.
But it was not too-- too noticeable.
Especially the-- in the [NON-ENGLISH], most of the doctors, the bankers, the-- the engineers, the businesspeople were Jewish.
And the certain way they had to have a certain relationship with the Jews.
And they had to, in a certain way, they admired the Jews.
Although they-- it was quite a lot of envy.
But in general, it was a pretty good relationship in our city.
Basically, yes.
The police officials, and there were some judges, and the fire department.
I mean, we have not had Jews in government, per se.
But they were pretty fair in our city.
Yeah, we had radios and most of-- we had a Jewish paper, which was not a daily.
It was like a-- a weekly paper, because the Jewish papers were edited in the capital, and we used to get it a few days, maybe a week later, because we didn't have any air transportation at that time.
And they used to come by the railroad-- by the train.
By the-- through the radio.
I remember we used to have in our house an old Marconi, Italian.
And in some occasions, based on the time difference, I used to listen to-- certain times, we used to catch the United States of America, which was quite interesting.
As time rolled, we start finding out many things, because we have had some refugees from Czechoslovakia who came to us.
And of course, later we had also some Polish refugees who fought the-- the Germans.
But that was at a later date.
And we tried to help the Jewish refugees that came from Czechoslovakia, because they came illegally, and we tried to provide them shelter and tried to make their stay legal.
And it was not easy, but we were able to do that.
We were actually, for some reason, we haven't been concerned, because we felt that Lithuania is a safe place.
It's a democratic country.
And we felt that-- we truthfully were shortsighted.
We didn't realize what actually-- what kind of storm is coming against us.

But especially when we had this opportunity to meet those who escaped from Czechoslovakia, this was a-- and we listened to their stories and to the stories of horror, we start thinking that times might be not as good in the future as they were, as we had them.
But still, we were entrenched in our country.
We have not-- never, we never had a thought that something terrible can happen to us.
Oh, yes.
We had many, many, many people-- I mean, many.
There were a number of people who used to emigrate.
We used to travel to Palestine.
I had some friends who went to Palestine at that time because the spirit of the Jewish, the Zionist movement was very strong in our city.
We had even some leaders from the Zionist movement who came to our-- to our country, and who tried to explain to us the importance of having our own country, our own state.
But unfortunately, we have-- we didn't have the time to accomplish.
Some people went to Israel, but most of them felt so secure in Lithuania in the sense that anti-Semitism was not so strong.
And this maybe was the reason why we felt that Lithuania is a good place to live.
And we had not seen, actually, the handwriting on the wall.
No, the borders were not open.
Lithuania was-- I mean, to emigrate, to go to Israel at that time, it was quite difficult.
You have to go through many procedures and through a long process in order to leave Lithuania.
Part of Lithuania was occupied by Russians.
As you know, I used to live in Kaunas, because I started in Kaunas.
And during the summer time, after school was finished, after the end of the school year, I used to go back to my family and spend summers in my native city with my friends, and so on.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was basically the way I-- I grew up part in Kaunas and part in Rakiszki with my family.
We have noticed and we felt that something is going on when the relations between Germany and Russia have started to come up in the newspapers, the articles about their approach.
And we have seen and felt that the proximity of Germany to Lithuania, which was just a matter of hours, we start feeling that there is some kind of danger, and something that might be affecting us.

And especially when the Russians have announced that they have made the Ribbentrop-- deal was made between Ribbentrop and Molotov, between Russia and the-- and the Nazi Germany, where they divided, partially, Germany took partially a part of Poland, and Lithuania as invaded, the three Baltic countries-- which is Lithuania, Latvia, and Estonia.

We actually start feeling that we are threatened, because suddenly, we saw what actually is happening to us and where-- the Russians who claimed that they are very prosperous, and they are very-- their country is-- that communism is-- is a great ideal.
We saw the way they came into Lithuania, and suddenly everything disappeared from the stores and from the factories, and Lithuania became a-- the economy has changed completely.
We have lost almost, I would say, right away the economic independence.
And the market, the price, the prices starts going up.
And the fact is that we lost also our-- our-- the value for money, which was the [NON-ENGLISH], was the Lithuanian money.
And it was changed to the Russian currency.
And it affected many merchants.
And especially in my father's business, and also most of the merchants who were dwelling in Lithuania.
No, you see, I was not at my-- at the city of [NON-ENGLISH], I was in Kovno studying in the Hebrew gymnasium.
And this was 1941, June 1941.
And I was ready-- we had finished the gymnasium, and ready to start my studies in the university.

My parents wanted to send me to Italy to study medicine, because the medical school in Lithuania was very weak in a certain way, and it was very difficult, especially for Jews, to get in the university.
So we start making arrangements.
And in order to go to Italy, I had to have my diploma from the gymnasium.
And this was one of the reasons that I have not traveled back to my city.
And I was waiting to get the diploma.
And we have finished our studies in the beginning of June.
And as days went by, and for some reason, the diploma was issued to the later part of June, June 20, June 19 or June 20.
We have also had, since the Russians came in, they have changed the political structure and the living structure in the country.

They have first of all assessed-- they start taking many people to-- to the-- to Siberia, those people who they felt they are bourgeois, that they are rich and they have businesses, and they're exploiters.
And by calling them bourgeois and exploiters, of the proletarians, they have taken out a big, large number of Jews whom they considered exploiters, and they took them to Siberia to get the families.
And their businesses were taken over by the Russians.
We, ourselves, in school, we had the opportunity in few occasions-- they would send us to nationalize some businesses, where we had to take inventories.
And unfortunately, we felt very bad for the owners who suddenly, they became just nobody.
They would not let them even into the business.
This happened also in the small villages.
My father, my family had also-- we were pretty wealthy of the standards at that time.
And my parents were also the least to being taken, taken-- taken away.
But in fact, my father was in a very excellent relationship with the-- with the clergy and some other friends.
And they have helped him to-- to be taken off the least at that time.
No, he served in the Lithuanian Army.
This was also a factor.
And he had many, many friends.
And they had prevented us to-- but we were not sure until the last minute.

So me being in waiting to get my diploma, and the fact also that in our house, where we lived in Kaunas, they have also assessed that each house, they felt that people should not occupy too much room.
They used to allocate an amount of square footage or square meters for each family.
So in our house, they have placed two Russian pilots who lived together with us.
And we had to give them, for free, a space.
And they used to live together.
And they were very nice and very friendly.
But suddenly on June 20, the night of June, 20, 1941, there was an alarm.
In the middle of the night, we heard the Russian pilots have-- were very disturbed, and they were being called, and they had to rush to the airport.
That same night, the airport from Kaunas, which was the capital of Lithuania, was bombed by the German aviation.
And unfortunately, the Russians didn't have time.

So now, I mean, this war started without any notification, without any advance or advice, or-- it was a-- a sudden decision by-- by the Nazi commanders, run by the Hitler regime, to-- to attack Russia.
And we were the first one being attacked, because Lithuania had a border with Germany.
So they have destroyed the few planes that Russia had.
And the fact also, Russia had some troops in Lithuania, especially in Kaunas.
But the troops were also not too well prepared.
They looked very good for the small Lithuanian country.
But in the general context, global context, they were not-- apparently not well prepared.
They didn't have even-- their-- their clothes and their military uniforms were also second- or third-rated.
So when the war started in June 21, the Russians panicked and they start looking for refuge.
And they start rushing with any type of vehicle that was available.
And some Jews were able also to mount them and to go deep back to Russia.
My position was to run also to-- to my city, but I found myself, after reaching the highway, that we were strifed by German planes.
And many of those people, civilians, who tried to run away and to go towards north, were killed and-- by the oncoming German-- German planes.
And I decided to go back to Kaunas and to be with my uncle, my grandmother, and my aunt.
And my little cousin was just born.
And I mention the cousin, because later on, I would have to explain to you about Harry.
So we found ourselves now, the radio blasted saying that Lithuanians, as you know, they were favoring-- they hated the Russians.
And they were favoring the Germans.
And right away, this elite of the Lithuanians start blasting through the radio, saying that they have noticed that the German-- victorious German troops are approaching Lithuania and the capital.
And they have heard that some Jewish-- the communists and Jews are trying to intervene and to obstruct the victorious entry of their German army.
And for each-- for each German or Lithuanian that will be killed by the communist and Jews, 100 Jews are going to be, at random, put to the wall and shot.
That's was actually the beginning-- the first news, which were every five minutes for the radio programs interrupted, and they used to have this announcement, which was made at that time.
As time went by, they started.
And as the Germans have come, as they came in-- but before the Germans came in, you should know that the Lithuanians became like beasts.
They have used every single opportunity to attack Jews, to kill them, to take away whatever their belongings.
And they, in different occasions, their murders was heinous, you know?
Regardless whether it was a young person or an old one, they used to attack them.
And they had the power.
There was no police or nobody to help or to bring order.
No order-- no-- no-- no enforcement.
And they had in their hands the justice, and the justice was done by them in the killing and plundering.
This went on even before the-- the-- the Germans came in.
As the Germans came in, they start regulating little by little.
But the regulation was-- right away, we noticed they were right away against the Jews.
The Jews, for instance, were not able to walk on the sidewalk.
They had to walk on the-- on the street.
The Jews had, in the beginning, right away, they had to turn in all their radios.
They had to turn into any kind of valuables.
They had to turn in any kind of currency.
They had to turn in any kind of-- if they had a stamp collection.
Like, I had an incredible stamp collection, because since a child, I collected stamps, and this was my hobby.
I had to take it over to the German or Lithuanian stations.
And little by little, they start pressuring the Jews and saying that they will find some kind of solution.
And the word was that they were going to place the Jews in a secluded section of Kaunas, which will be called the ghetto.
And they will give them a certain time to enter the ghetto, and this is an order which has to be followed and obeyed.
